saida_bruta:
There was nothing heroic in the appearance of the man waking on the sleeping-porch of his Dutch Colonial home, located in Zenith’s respectable suburb of Floral Heights.

His name was George F. Babbitt. Now forty-six in this April of 1920, he produced nothing tangible—neither shoes, nor butter, nor poetry—but he was highly skilled at persuading people to buy houses they could not afford.

His large head was pink, topped with thin, dry brown hair. In sleep, his face appeared babyish, despite his wrinkles and the red indents left by eyeglasses on the bridge of his nose. Though not obese, he was remarkably well-fed; his cheeks were fleshy pads, and the smooth, helpless hand resting on his khaki blanket was slightly puffed. He looked prosperous, thoroughly married, and entirely unromantic. Equally unromantic was his sleeping-porch, which overlooked a single elm, two tidy lawns, a concrete driveway, and a corrugated iron garage. Yet Babbitt was once more dreaming of the fairy child, a vision far more romantic than scarlet pagodas by a silver sea.

saida_bruta:
No quality of heroism belonged to the man waking on the sleeping-porch of his Dutch Colonial house in Floral Heights, a genteel suburb of Zenith. 

He was George F. Babbitt. At forty-six, in the April of 1920, his daily efforts created nothing of physical substance—neither boots, nor bread, nor verses—yet he was exceptionally skilled at convincing people to purchase houses beyond their means. 

His broad, pink head was crowned with coarse, thinning brown hair. While sleeping, his face looked childlike notwithstanding his wrinkles and the deep red indentations left by eyeglasses on his nose. Not altogether obese, he was undeniably prosperous; his cheeks formed heavy cushions, and the soft, vulnerable hand resting on his khaki comforter was somewhat puffy. He appeared comfortable, deeply married, and completely devoid of spirituality. Equally devoid of spirit was the sleeping-porch itself, which looked out upon a solitary elm, two trimmed lawns, a cement driveway, and a tin garage. Yet Babbitt was, at this moment, dreaming of the fairy girl, an image vastly more enchanting than crimson temples beside a silver ocean.

In sleep, his face appeared strangely childlike despite his wrinkles and the deep red marks left by glasses on the bridge of his nose. He seemed comfortable, thoroughly married, and altogether unspiritual. Equally lacking in spirit was the sleeping-porch, which overlooked a single elm, two manicured lawns, a concrete driveway, and a tin garage. Yet Babbitt was, at that instant, dreaming of the fairy child, a fantasy far more enchanting than crimson temples beside a silver sea.
